title: Research Programme
text:
Eyewitness evidence remains one of the most influential forms of evidence in criminal investigations and criminal trials. However, eyewitness accuracy varies substantially across individuals, witnessing conditions, and decision contexts.
While decades of research have identified factors associated with eyewitness error, courts and investigators are still largely required to evaluate eyewitness evidence qualitatively rather than quantitatively. The central challenge underpinning this programme is therefore:

How can we estimate the reliability of eyewitness evidence?

Addressing this challenge requires understanding the mechanisms that influence eyewitness accuracy, developing computational models capable of integrating those mechanisms, and determining how such information can be communicated effectively within legal settings.

title: Research Programme
text:
Eyewitness evidence remains one of the most influential forms of evidence in criminal investigations and criminal trials. However, eyewitness accuracy varies substantially across individuals, witnessing conditions, and decision contexts.
While decades of research have identified factors associated with eyewitness error, courts and investigators are still largely required to evaluate eyewitness evidence qualitatively rather than quantitatively. The central challenge underpinning this programme is therefore:

How can we estimate the reliability of eyewitness evidence?

Addressing this challenge requires understanding the mechanisms that influence eyewitness accuracy, developing computational models capable of integrating those mechanisms, and determining how such information can be communicated effectively within legal settings.

title: Research Programme
text:
Research Vision
The long-term aim of this programme is to develop calibrated, interpretable, and empirically grounded methods for estimating eyewitness reliability.
The programme integrates memory science, decision science, metacognition, computational modelling, and legal decision-making research. Together, these approaches seek to move beyond identifying factors that influence eyewitness performance towards estimating the probability that an eyewitness identification is correct under specified conditions.

Research Programme Roadmap

title: Research Programme
text:
Theme 1: Witnessing Conditions

Research Question
How do witnessing conditions influence eyewitness reliability?


Focus
This theme investigates how variables such as retention interval, viewing distance, exposure duration, stress, lighting, and environmental conditions influence eyewitness memory and identification performance.


Current Work

Forgetting curve modelling
Viewing distance study
Exposure duration study
Estimator variable modelling

title: Research Programme
text:
Theme 2: Evidence Accumulation

Research Question
How is evidence accumulated and transformed into identification decisions?


Focus
This theme applies computational approaches to understand the cognitive processes underlying eyewitness decisions. Particular emphasis is placed on modelling uncertainty, evidence quality, and decision formation.


Current Work

Bayesian modelling
Drift diffusion modelling
Evidence accumulation frameworks
Computational modelling of identification decisions

title: Research Programme
text:
Theme 3: Confidence & Metacognition

Research Question
When and why does confidence predict accuracy?


Focus
This theme investigates how eyewitnesses monitor and evaluate their own memory, and whether confidence judgments provide meaningful information regarding identification accuracy.


Current Work

Confidence–accuracy relationships
Confidence calibration
Metacognitive sensitivity
Confidence under varying witnessing conditions

title: Research Programme
text:
Theme 4: Individual Differences

Research Question
Why are some individuals more accurate eyewitnesses than others?


Focus
This theme examines cognitive, perceptual, and metacognitive factors that may explain individual variation in eyewitness performance.


Future Work

Cognitive predictors of eyewitness performance
Memory ability and identification accuracy
Perceptual expertise
Metacognitive individual differences

title: Research Programme
text:
Theme 5: Ecological Validity & Virtual Reality

Research Question
Do laboratory findings generalise to realistic eyewitness experiences?


Focus
This theme seeks to increase the realism of eyewitness research through immersive and naturalistic methodologies while maintaining experimental control.


Current Work (in development)

Virtual reality eyewitness paradigms
Naturalistic witnessing environments
Presence and realism research
Behavioural and emotional responses in VR

title: Research Programme
text:
Theme 6: Jury Decision Making

Research Question
How can scientific evidence regarding eyewitness reliability be communicated effectively to legal decision makers?


Focus
This theme examines how jurors and other legal decision makers interpret eyewitness evidence and how uncertainty can be communicated in a transparent and understandable manner.


Future Work

Probability communication
Reliability estimation frameworks
Decision support approaches
Jury interpretation of eyewitness evidence

title: Publications
text: This research programme seeks to understand how eyewitness evidence is generated, how its reliability can be estimated, and how that information can be interpreted within legal decision-making contexts.

title: Publications
text:
Published Research

Published

Temporal Dynamics of Eyewitness Memory: Evidence for a Multiphase Forgetting Curve From Minutes to Months
Farmer, Turner, & Kaye (2026)
Applied Cognitive Psychology
Summary
This study investigated eyewitness memory retention from minutes to months and identified a three-phase pattern of forgetting consisting of rapid early forgetting, a period of relative stability, and gradual longer-term decline.
Programme Contribution
Provides foundational evidence regarding how memory decay influences eyewitness reliability.

title: Publications
text:
Research Under Review

Under Review

Estimating Eyewitness Identification Accuracy Across Witnessing Conditions: A Probabilistic Modelling Approach
Farmer et al.
Under review at the Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition
Summary
This paper develops calibrated probabilistic models that estimate eyewitness identification accuracy across varying witnessing conditions, combining retention interval, viewing distance, and exposure duration.
Programme Contribution
Provides the probabilistic estimation framework that underpins the broader goal of computational estimation of eyewitness reliability.



Under Review

Evidence Diagnosticity as a Common Computational Currency in Eyewitness Identification
Farmer et al.
Under review at Memory & Cognition
Summary
This paper applies drift diffusion modelling to eyewitness identification decisions to investigate how witnessing conditions and confidence influence evidence accumulation.
Programme Contribution
Provides a computational framework linking witnessing conditions, confidence, response time, and identification accuracy.

title: Publications
text:
Papers in Development

In Development

Confidence and Metacognition in Eyewitness Identification
Summary
This paper investigates confidence calibration and metacognitive sensitivity under varying witnessing conditions.
Programme Contribution
Examines how witnesses evaluate their own memory and the circumstances under which confidence can inform reliability assessments.



In Development

Reassessing the Turnbull Safeguard: Juror Decision-Making and the Evaluation of Identification Evidence
Summary
This paper examines the Turnbull direction through the lens of cognitive psychology, drawing on research on juror decision-making, narrative construction, heuristic reasoning, and belief revision.
Programme Contribution
Extends the programme from estimating eyewitness reliability to understanding how eyewitness evidence is interpreted and evaluated within legal decision-making contexts.

title: Publications
text:
Research Trajectory
Collectively, these publications contribute to a broader programme seeking to understand how eyewitness evidence is generated, how its reliability can be estimated, and how uncertainty can be communicated within legal settings.

title: Projects
text: The projects below contribute to the broader goal of developing scientifically grounded methods for quantifying, estimating, and communicating eyewitness reliability.

title: Projects
text:
Current Research

Completed

Eyewitness Forgetting and Retention Interval Modelling
Research Question
How does eyewitness accuracy change over time?
Overview
This project investigates how retention interval influences eyewitness identification accuracy across timescales ranging from minutes to months.
Contribution
Provides foundational evidence regarding how memory decay influences eyewitness reliability.



Ongoing

Estimating Eyewitness Identification Accuracy Across Witnessing Conditions
Research Question
Can eyewitness identification accuracy be estimated probabilistically across varying witnessing conditions?
Overview
This project develops calibrated probabilistic models of eyewitness identification accuracy across conditions including retention interval, separation distance, and exposure duration.
Contribution
Provides the probabilistic estimation framework that underpins the broader goal of computational estimation of eyewitness reliability.



Ongoing

Evidence Diagnosticity as a Common Computational Currency in Eyewitness Identification
Research Question
Can diverse influences on eyewitness performance be understood through a common computational measure of evidence diagnosticity?
Overview
This project applies drift diffusion modelling to eyewitness decisions to examine how witnessing conditions and confidence influence evidence accumulation, response time, and identification accuracy.
Contribution
Provides a computational framework linking witnessing conditions, confidence, and identification decisions.



Ongoing

Confidence and Metacognition in Eyewitness Identification
Research Question
When and why does confidence predict eyewitness accuracy?
Overview
This project investigates confidence calibration and metacognitive sensitivity under varying witnessing conditions.
Contribution
Examines how witnesses evaluate their own memory and when confidence can inform reliability assessments.



Ongoing

Juror Decision-Making and Communication of Eyewitness Reliability
Research Question
How can eyewitness reliability and uncertainty be communicated effectively to legal decision-makers?
Overview
This research theme examines how jurors evaluate eyewitness evidence, how confidence and reliability information influence judgments, and how scientific evidence regarding eyewitness accuracy can be communicated effectively within legal settings.
Current work includes the evaluation of the Turnbull safeguard and broader investigations of juror reasoning, evidence interpretation, and decision-making.
Contribution
Provides the bridge between computational estimation of eyewitness reliability and its practical application within the justice system.

title: Projects
text:

title: Projects
text:
Programme Integration
Together, these projects form a coherent programme progressing from the study of witnessing conditions and memory processes, through computational modelling of eyewitness decisions and confidence, towards increasingly realistic witnessing environments and ultimately the communication of reliability estimates to legal decision-makers.

title: Impact & Funding
text: This research programme is designed to connect basic psychological science with practical questions about how eyewitness evidence is evaluated in legal contexts.

title: Impact & Funding
text:
Practical Impact
Eyewitness identification evidence can have substantial consequences for criminal investigations, prosecutions, and jury decision-making. However, courts and investigators often face the challenge of evaluating eyewitness evidence under conditions of uncertainty.
This programme aims to support more informed evaluation of eyewitness evidence by developing scientifically grounded approaches for estimating reliability, communicating uncertainty, and improving decision-making.

title: Impact & Funding
text:
Legal and Policy Relevance
The programme has direct relevance to questions concerning:

eyewitness identification procedures
judicial guidance on identification evidence
juror interpretation of eyewitness testimony
communication of uncertainty in criminal trials
evidence-based reform of legal safeguards
Investigations

A central long-term aim is to contribute to guidance that is psychologically informed, empirically grounded, and usable within real legal settings.

title: Impact & Funding
text:
Methodological Contributions
The programme also contributes methodologically by integrating:

probabilistic modelling
evidence accumulation models
confidence calibration
metacognitive analysis
immersive virtual reality methods
applied jury decision-making research

Together, these approaches provide a framework for moving beyond qualitative descriptions of eyewitness risk factors towards calibrated estimates of reliability.

title: Impact & Funding
text:
Funding Potential
The programme is well positioned for future funding in areas such as:

memory and cognition
legal psychology
criminal justice
computational social science
artificial intelligence and decision support
immersive technologies and virtual reality
evidence communication and public policy

Future grant applications could develop from several strands of the programme, including computational estimation of eyewitness reliability, VR-based eyewitness research, individual differences in eyewitness accuracy, and juror interpretation of probabilistic evidence.

title: Impact & Funding
text:
Pathway to Impact
The long-term pathway to impact involves:

identifying factors that influence eyewitness reliability
developing models that estimate accuracy under specified conditions
validating these models in increasingly realistic settings
testing how reliability estimates are understood by legal decision-makers
translating findings into guidance, tools, and policy-relevant recommendations

title: Impact & Funding
text:
Long-Term Impact Goal
The ultimate impact goal is to improve how eyewitness evidence is interpreted in criminal justice settings by developing transparent, explainable, and empirically grounded methods for estimating and communicating eyewitness reliability.

title: Computational Estimation of Eyewitness Reliability
text:
A research programme integrating memory science, decision science, and computational modelling to develop scientifically grounded methods for estimating eyewitness reliability and communicating uncertainty within legal decision-making contexts.



Grand Challenge
How can eyewitness reliability be quantified, estimated, and communicated?
This programme addresses the challenge of moving from qualitative judgments about eyewitness evidence towards calibrated, interpretable, and empirically grounded estimates of reliability.

Research Programme Projects Publications

title: Computational Estimation of Eyewitness Reliability
text:
The Challenge
Eyewitness evidence remains one of the most influential forms of evidence in criminal investigations and criminal trials. However, eyewitness accuracy can vary substantially as a function of witnessing conditions, memory processes, confidence judgments, and decision-making factors.
Current legal guidance identifies factors associated with eyewitness error, but provides limited means of estimating the likely reliability of a particular identification. Consequently, judges, juries, investigators, and legal practitioners are often required to evaluate eyewitness evidence using qualitative assessments rather than quantitative estimates of reliability.

title: Computational Estimation of Eyewitness Reliability
text:
Research Vision
The long-term aim of this research programme is to develop calibrated, interpretable, and empirically grounded methods for estimating eyewitness reliability.
By integrating experimental psychology, computational modelling, metacognition, and legal decision-making research, the programme seeks to move beyond identifying factors that influence accuracy towards estimating the probability that an eyewitness identification is correct under specified conditions.
The ultimate goal is to develop scientifically grounded frameworks that support the interpretation of eyewitness evidence while remaining transparent, explainable, and suitable for real-world legal contexts.

Research Themes




Witnessing Conditions


Distance, retention interval, exposure duration, stress, and environmental factors.




Evidence Accumulation


Bayesian modelling, drift diffusion models, and computational estimation.




Confidence & Metacognition


Confidence calibration, metacognitive sensitivity, and self-monitoring.




Individual Differences


Why some individuals are consistently more accurate eyewitnesses than others.




Ecological Validity & VR


Immersive and naturalistic eyewitness research.




Jury Decision Making


Communication of reliability and interpretation of evidence.




Current Research




Eyewitness Forgetting


Published research examining memory retention from minutes to months.




Probabilistic Modelling


Bayesian modelling of eyewitness identification accuracy across witnessing conditions.




Evidence Diagnosticity


Drift diffusion modelling of confidence, response time, and accuracy.




Metacognition


Understanding confidence calibration and metacognitive sensitivity.




Turnbull Safeguard


Evaluating juror decision-making and judicial guidance on identification evidence.




Emerging Research Themes




Individual Differences


Investigating why some people are more accurate eyewitnesses than others.




Ecological Validity & VR


Developing immersive and ecologically valid eyewitness paradigms.

title: Computational Estimation of Eyewitness Reliability
text:
Long-Term Objective
To develop computationally informed, empirically calibrated approaches capable of providing interpretable estimates of eyewitness reliability that can inform research, policy, and legal decision making.

title: About
text:
I am a psychology lecturer and researcher whose work focuses on eyewitness identification, memory, confidence, decision-making, and the application of computational approaches to legal evidence.
My research seeks to understand how eyewitness evidence is generated, how its reliability can be estimated, and how uncertainty can be communicated within legal decision-making contexts.
A central aim of this work is to move beyond qualitative assessments of eyewitness evidence towards calibrated, interpretable, and empirically grounded estimates of reliability.

title: About
text:
Current Research Programme
The current programme integrates several complementary strands of research:

Eyewitness memory and forgetting
Witnessing conditions and identification accuracy
Probabilistic modelling
Evidence accumulation and decision processes
Confidence and metacognition
Juror decision-making
Ecological validity and virtual reality

Together, these themes contribute towards the long-term goal of developing scientifically grounded methods for quantifying, estimating, and communicating eyewitness reliability.

title: About
text:
Research Philosophy
My approach combines basic psychological science with applied legal questions.
I am particularly interested in research that not only explains cognitive processes but also has the potential to improve real-world decision-making. This includes developing models that are transparent, interpretable, and capable of supporting the evaluation of evidence in practical settings.

title: About
text:
Teaching
Alongside research, I teach undergraduate and postgraduate psychology, with particular interests in cognitive psychology and quantitative research methods, but I have regularly taught all BPS core modules, and others including forensic psychology and cyberpsychology.
My teaching philosophy emphasises making lessons fun and engaging, confidence-building, accessibility, and active discussion.
I receive consistent excellent feedback from students, and compared to peers, mostly achieve the highest average grades across the modules I teach.

title: About
text:
Future Directions
Future work will extend the programme through research on individual differences in eyewitness performance, immersive virtual reality methodologies, and the communication of probabilistic evidence to legal decision-makers.

title: About
text:
Contact
For research collaborations, or general enquiries, please get in touch. sfarmer@arden.ac.uk

title: Research Programme
section: Theme 2: Computational Modelling of Eyewitness Decisions
text:
Theme 2: Computational Modelling of Eyewitness Decisions

Research Question
How can eyewitness identification decisions be modelled computationally?


Focus
This theme develops computational accounts of eyewitness decision-making. Current work uses Bayesian modelling and drift diffusion models to investigate how witnessing conditions, confidence, response time, and identification accuracy are related.
The aim is to move beyond describing factors that influence eyewitness performance towards developing calibrated and interpretable models capable of estimating eyewitness reliability under specified conditions.


Current Work

Bayesian modelling of eyewitness identification accuracy
Drift diffusion modelling of evidence accumulation
Evidence diagnosticity as a computational summary of decision quality
Modelling confidence, response time, and accuracy
Calibration and predictive model evaluation
